preamble: The transformation of the alveolar nasal /n/ into the close front unrounded vowel /i/ is a documented phonetic shift, notably observed in the historical development of Ancient Egyptian. This process, often involving an intermediate stage of nasalization, represents a vocalization of the consonant.

examples.note: The word 'inb.t' (she-caracal) from the Old Kingdom shows a shift to 'ib.t'. Another instance is the writing 'ir' for 'inr' (stone) in the Graeco-Roman period, where /n/ assimilated to /i/.

sources: Bojowald, S. (2021). The Egyptian Phonetic Change between "n" and "i". Athens Journal of Philology, 8(2), 117-128.
